common.foot1: Cobijo Health — a California nonprofit (501(c)(3) in formation). This tool provides information, not legal or medical advice. Free & open-source. · hello@cobijohealth.org
common.mt_note: This page was translated with machine assistance and is being reviewed by native speakers. The English version is authoritative. Spot an error? Email hello@cobijohealth.org.

about.title: About Cobijo Health — who we are & how it works
about.meta_desc: Cobijo Health is a California nonprofit (501(c)(3) in formation) building a free, multilingual tool that helps patients get financial help for medical bills. Who we are, how it works, and where our data comes from.
about.lead: Cobijo Health is a California nonprofit (501(c)(3) in formation) building a free, multilingual tool that helps low-income, uninsured, and immigrant patients get the financial help they're legally entitled to for their medical bills.
about.mission_p: Every California hospital is required by law — the Hospital Fair Pricing Act — to offer free or discounted care to patients who can't afford their bills. Nonprofit hospitals must do the same under federal law (IRS §501(r)). But the help is effectively hidden: each hospital's rules sit in a separate, dense legal PDF, rarely translated and never in one place. People who qualify get sent to collections instead. Cobijo's job is to be the front door to help you're already owed.
about.how_p: You tell us your hospital, income, and household size. In seconds, Cobijo tells you whether you may qualify for free or discounted care at that specific hospital — with the income limit — screens you for Medi-Cal and Covered California, explains your rights if a bill is in collections, and writes you a ready-to-send request letter. It's free, it works in many languages, and it saves nothing you enter.
about.ai_h: How we use AI (and where humans stay in charge)
about.ai_p: The reason this can exist is AI. We use large language models to read hundreds of hospitals' financial-assistance policy PDFs and pull each one's exact eligibility rules into a single, clean, open dataset. AI does the reading and drafting; people verify flagged edge cases. Your eligibility for public benefits is computed with open-source public-benefit rules (PolicyEngine), not guessed. AI helps — it never makes the final decision for you, and only the hospital can approve your assistance.
about.src1: California's official hospital financial-assistance policy registry (HCAI)
about.src2: California's Hospital Fair Pricing Act (Health & Safety Code §127400 et seq.)
about.src3: Federal charity-care requirements for nonprofit hospitals (IRS §501(r))
about.src4: Medi-Cal and Covered California eligibility rules (via PolicyEngine)
about.src_note: Everything Cobijo builds — the dataset and the tools — is open-source and open-data, so anyone can check our work or build on it.
about.team_p: Cobijo Health was founded by Ian Duncan, who builds the product end to end. We're recruiting an independent founding board — a nonprofit/health attorney, a CPA/treasurer, a healthcare-access expert, a fundraiser, and a community member with lived experience — and filing for 501(c)(3) status. If you'd like to help, we'd love to hear from you.
about.involve_lead: Cobijo Health is early, open, and built in the open. There are three ways to help — all real, none requiring money.
about.involve_board_p: We're recruiting five independent directors as we file for 501(c)(3) status: a nonprofit or health attorney, a CPA or treasurer, a healthcare-access expert, a fundraiser, and a community member with lived experience of medical debt. A few hours a quarter, real impact. Email us.
about.involve_help_p: We especially need native speakers to review our translations — we serve 10 languages, currently machine-assisted. We'd also love to hear from clinics, legal-aid groups, and community organizations who reach the patients we're built for. Email us.
about.involve_support_p: Everything — the tools and the dataset — is free and open-source. Star or contribute to the project on GitHub — it's a genuine public good, and every bit of visibility helps patients find it.
about.disc_card: Cobijo Health gives you information, not legal, medical, financial, or tax advice. Results are estimates based on what you enter and public hospital policies — they are not a decision or a guarantee. Only the hospital can decide if you qualify. Cobijo is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or acting on behalf of any hospital.

privacy.title: Privacy — Cobijo Health
privacy.meta_desc: Cobijo Health does not save your answers. What you enter is used only to calculate your result, in the moment, and is never stored, sold, or shared.
privacy.summary: Short version: We don't save your answers. Ever.
privacy.intro: Cobijo Health is a free tool that helps you find out if you may qualify for hospital charity care, and creates a request letter you can use. We built it to protect your privacy.
privacy.collect_p: To give you a result, we ask for your hospital, your income, your household size, whether you have insurance, and whether a bill is in collections.
privacy.use_p: We use your answers only to calculate your result and letter, right then, in the moment. As soon as we show you the result, your answers are gone.
privacy.dont1: We don't save your answers in any database.
privacy.dont2: We don't create an account or ask for your name.
privacy.dont3: We don't sell, rent, or share your answers with anyone.
privacy.dont4: We will never share what you enter with your hospital, the government, or immigration.
privacy.hipaa_p: Cobijo Health is not a doctor, hospital, or insurance company. That means HIPAA — the medical privacy law — does not apply to this tool. We protect you a different, stronger way: by not storing your answers at all.
privacy.analytics_p: We use privacy-friendly analytics (Plausible) to count how many people visit our pages. It uses no cookies and collects no personal information. It cannot see your form answers.
privacy.third_p: Our website is delivered through standard hosting and security services (such as Cloudflare). These help the site load safely. They do not receive your form answers.
privacy.changes_p: If we change this policy, we'll update it on this page and change the date below.
privacy.nonprofit_note: Cobijo Health is a California nonprofit (501(c)(3) in formation).

faq.title: FAQ — Cobijo Health
faq.meta_desc: Answers to common questions about Cobijo Health: Is it free? Is my information safe? Will getting help affect my immigration case? What if I'm undocumented or my bill is in collections?
faq.intro: Honest answers to the questions people ask most. Still stuck? Email hello@cobijohealth.org.
faq.a1: Yes. Cobijo Health is completely free to use, with no catch. We're a nonprofit, not a business — we don't charge you, and we don't take a cut of anything you save. There's nothing to buy and no account to create.
faq.q2: Is my information safe? Do you save what I enter?
faq.a2: We don't save your answers at all. What you type is used only to calculate your result in the moment, then it's gone — there's no account, no database of your answers, and we never sell or share them. We will never share what you enter with your hospital, the government, or immigration. See our Privacy page for details.
faq.q3: Will getting help hurt my immigration case ("public charge")?
faq.a3: For most people, no. Under current federal rules, using Medi-Cal, CHIP, Covered California, emergency care, or a hospital's financial-assistance (charity care) program does not count against you in a "public charge" immigration test — and hospital charity care is not a government benefit at all. Public charge also does not apply to many people, including green-card renewals, citizenship applicants, and refugee, asylee, U-visa, T-visa, and VAWA cases. This is general information, not legal advice, and immigration rules can change. For your specific situation, please talk to a trusted immigration attorney or a nonprofit legal-aid organization before making a decision.
faq.q4: Can I use this if I'm undocumented?
faq.a4: Yes. Hospital charity care is based on income and household size, not immigration status, and California hospitals must offer it to everyone who qualifies. In California, Medi-Cal is also available to income-eligible people regardless of immigration status, though enrollment rules can change over time. We don't ask for your immigration status and we don't save your answers.
faq.q5: How accurate is this? Is it a guarantee?
faq.a5: Cobijo gives you an estimate based on the information you enter and each hospital's own published financial-assistance policy. It is not a decision and not a guarantee. Only the hospital can approve your assistance — always confirm with the hospital's financial-assistance office. If we ever aren't sure about a hospital's policy, we tell you.
faq.q6: What if my bill already went to collections?
faq.a6: You may still have options. In many cases you can apply for charity care even after a bill has gone to collections, and California law gives you rights when a hospital bill is being collected. Cobijo explains those rights and can point you to free legal aid.
faq.q7: Do I need insurance to use this?
faq.a7: No. Cobijo is built especially for uninsured and self-pay patients, but insured patients with high out-of-pocket bills can qualify for help too. Enter your situation and we'll tell you what may apply.
faq.q8: Who's behind Cobijo, and is it a real nonprofit?
faq.a8: Cobijo Health was founded by Ian Duncan and is a California nonprofit in the process of forming (501(c)(3) status pending). It's open-source and built in the public interest. Learn more on our About page.
faq.a9: Cobijo works in English, Spanish, Chinese, Vietnamese, Tagalog, Korean, Armenian, Farsi, Arabic, and Russian. Use the language menu at the top of any page to switch.

plan.cc_free: You likely qualify for **FREE care**. {name} gives free/full charity care up to {free:.0f}% of the Federal Poverty Level{upto}, and your household is at ~{pct:.0f}%.
plan.cc_free_upto: (free for a household of {household} earning up to ~${ceil:,}/yr)
plan.cc_discount_tier: You likely qualify for a **DISCOUNT**. Your ~{pct:.0f}% FPL falls in this hospital's {lo:.0f}–{hi:.0f}% band: {how}.
plan.cc_discount_ceiling: You're within {name}'s discount-eligible range (up to {disc_ceiling:.0f}% FPL) — apply for the sliding-scale discount.
plan.cc_high_cost: You have insurance, but {name} has a **high-medical-cost** provision: if your out-of-pocket bills exceed ~{thr:.0f}% of your income you may still qualify for help. Worth applying.
plan.cc_over: Your income (~{pct:.0f}% FPL) is above {name}'s assistance ceiling (~{ceiling:.0f}% FPL) — but still apply (policies changed in 2025), and use bill-error review, negotiation, and the options below.
plan.ben_insured: You reported having insurance — we'd still check for cost-sharing help and secondary programs.
plan.ben_medicaid_heuristic: **Medi-Cal (California Medicaid)** — at ~{pct:.0f}% FPL you're likely income-eligible for free or low-cost coverage that can even apply to recent bills. California covers many adults regardless of immigration status, though enrollment rules changed in 2026 — check your current eligibility. Using Medi-Cal does not count against a public-charge immigration test.
plan.ben_aca_heuristic: **Covered California (ACA) subsidies** — at ~{pct:.0f}% FPL you likely qualify for premium help. (Enhanced subsidies were set to lapse after 2025 — verify 2026 rules; PolicyEngine reflects current law.)
plan.ben_over_heuristic: Income may be above Medi-Cal/subsidy lines — we'd still check county indigent-care and special programs.
plan.pe_medicaid: **Medi-Cal (California Medicaid)** — current CA rules (via PolicyEngine) show your household likely eligible for free/low-cost coverage worth an estimated ~${value:,}/yr. California covers many adults regardless of immigration status (enrollment rules changed in 2026 — check your current eligibility), and coverage can apply to recent bills. Using Medi-Cal does not count against a public-charge test.
plan.pe_aca: **Covered California (ACA) subsidies** — PolicyEngine estimates a premium tax credit of ~${ptc:,}/yr to lower your monthly premium on a marketplace plan.
plan.pe_over: Based on current CA rules (PolicyEngine), your income appears above the Medi-Cal and marketplace-subsidy lines — we'd still check county indigent-care and special programs.
plan.debt_rights: Because a bill is in collections: you have rights under the FDCPA and CA law — you can dispute the debt and request validation.
plan.debt_retroactive: Charity care can often be applied **retroactively** to wipe a bill already in collections — do that first.
plan.debt_legalaid: For any lawsuit or wage-garnishment threat, we'll connect you to **free legal aid** (we don't give legal advice).
plan.debt_credit_note: Note (2026): the federal rule removing medical debt from credit reports was struck down, but bureaus still drop paid collections and those under $500.
plan.plan_greeting: Hi {first} — here's your personalized plan for the bill from {name}.

plan.step1_apply_nophone: We'll generate the request letter for you (below) — send it to the hospital's Financial Assistance / Business Office.

landing.title: Cobijo Health — free help with your medical bills
landing.meta_desc: Cobijo Health is a free, multilingual tool that helps low-income and uninsured patients in California get financial help for medical bills: hospital charity care, benefit screening, and medical-debt defense.
landing.h1: Millions of Californians qualify for free hospital care — and never get it.
landing.lead: Every California hospital is required by law to offer free or discounted care to patients who can't afford their bills. Cobijo Health finds that help for you — in plain language, in your language, for free.
landing.free: Free · multilingual · nothing is saved · not legal or medical advice
landing.how_h: Three ways we help with one bill
landing.how_s: Tell us your hospital, income, and household size. In seconds you get a personalized plan.
landing.st1_p: Find out if you qualify for free or discounted care at that specific hospital — with the exact income limit — and get a ready-to-send request letter.
landing.st2_p: We screen you for Medi-Cal and Covered California — coverage you may qualify for that can even apply to recent bills.
landing.st3_p: Know your rights if a bill is in collections, apply charity care retroactively, and get connected to free legal aid.
landing.who_s: The people the system leaves to decode a 20-page policy alone.
landing.who1: Uninsured and self-pay patients facing a bill they can't afford
landing.who2: Low-income and immigrant families — California covers eligible adults regardless of immigration status
landing.who3: Anyone whose bill went to collections before they knew help existed
landing.who4: Patients who've never seen this explained in their own language
landing.moat_p: Every hospital's charity-care rules are buried in a separate, dense legal PDF. No one had ever put them in one place. Cobijo uses AI to read California hospitals' financial-assistance policies and extract each one's exact rules into one clean, open dataset — so the tool can tell you what your hospital actually offers. Open-source and open-data, built in the public interest.
landing.band_h: Ready to see what you qualify for?
landing.band_p: It takes about a minute, it's completely free, and we don't save anything you enter.
landing.tagline: This tool provides information, not legal or medical advice. Free to use. Open-source.

guide_afford.title: Can't Afford Your Hospital Bill in California? Your Options | Cobijo Health
guide_afford.meta: Can't afford a hospital bill in California? You may qualify for free or discounted care under state law — even if the bill is old or already in collections. Here's what to do.
guide_afford.h1: Can't afford your hospital bill in California? Here's what to do
guide_afford.lead: If a hospital bill is more than you can pay, you are not out of options — and you are not alone. California law requires hospitals to offer free or discounted care to patients who can't afford their bills. Here's how to get the help you may already be owed.
guide_afford.s1_h: First: don't ignore the bill, but don't rush to pay it either
guide_afford.s1_p: Many people who could have their bill reduced or erased end up paying in full — or getting sent to collections — simply because no one told them help existed. Before you pay, put it on a credit card, or agree to a payment plan, find out whether you qualify for financial assistance. Applying costs nothing and does not hurt your credit.
guide_afford.s2_h: You may qualify for free or discounted care
guide_afford.s2_p: Under California's Hospital Fair Pricing Act (Health & Safety Code §127400 and following), hospitals must offer charity care (free care) and discounted care to eligible patients. Depending on the hospital, patients with household income up to 400% of the Federal Poverty Level — roughly $60,000 a year for one person or about $125,000 for a family of four — can qualify for a discount, and lower-income patients often pay nothing at all. Being uninsured, or having medical bills that are high compared to your income, can also make you eligible.
guide_afford.s3_h: It doesn't matter if the bill is old or already in collections
guide_afford.s3_p: You can apply for financial assistance even after you've received the bill, set up a payment plan, or had the account sent to a collection agency. In California, a hospital generally cannot report your bill to a credit agency or sell the debt until at least 180 days after the first bill (AB 1020). And under a 2024 law (SB 1061), medical debt cannot be reported on your California credit report at all.
guide_afford.s4_h: How Cobijo can help — free, in your language
guide_afford.s4_p: Cobijo Health is a free, nonprofit tool. Tell us your hospital, income, and household size, and we'll tell you whether you may qualify for free or discounted care at that specific hospital, screen you for Medi-Cal and Covered California, and write you a ready-to-send request letter. We don't save anything you enter.
guide_afford.li1: Find your hospital's financial-assistance policy (Cobijo has every California hospital).
guide_afford.li2: Check the income limit for your household size — you may qualify for free or discounted care.
guide_afford.li3: Ask the hospital's billing or financial-assistance office for the application form.
guide_afford.li4: Apply even if the bill is old or in collections — and keep a copy of everything.
guide_afford.li5: Also check whether you qualify for Medi-Cal or Covered California.
guide_afford.cta_p: Answer a few quick questions and get a personalized plan plus a ready-to-send letter — free, in your language, nothing saved.

guide_apply.title: How to Apply for Hospital Charity Care in California (Step by Step) | Cobijo Health
guide_apply.meta: A step-by-step guide to applying for hospital charity care and financial assistance in California: who qualifies, what documents you need, and how to submit — free.
guide_apply.h1: How to apply for hospital charity care, step by step
guide_apply.lead: Charity care is free or discounted hospital care for patients who can't afford their bills. Every California hospital must offer it. Here's exactly how to apply — and what to have ready.
guide_apply.s1_p: Charity care and discounts are based mainly on your household income and size. Under California's Hospital Fair Pricing Act, hospitals must offer free care to the lowest-income patients and discounted care to patients with income up to 400% of the Federal Poverty Level. Being uninsured or having unusually high medical bills relative to your income can also qualify you. Each hospital sets its own income limits within the law — Cobijo shows you the exact limit for your hospital.
guide_apply.s2_p: Call the hospital's billing or financial-assistance office (sometimes called patient financial services) and say you'd like to apply for financial assistance, or charity care, for a hospital bill. Ask them to send you the application form and a list of the documents they need. Hospitals are required to give you a written application and a plain-language summary of their policy, and to help you if you ask.
guide_apply.s3_p: Most hospitals ask for proof of income and household size. Common documents include recent pay stubs, a tax return or W-2, a bank statement, or a written statement if you have no income. You generally do not need to prove immigration status to get charity care, and applying will not affect your immigration case. If you're missing a document, ask — many hospitals accept a signed statement.
guide_apply.s4_p: Send the completed application and documents to the address or email the hospital gives you, and keep a copy of everything. Ask for written confirmation that they received it and when you'll hear back. If you're denied, you can ask why in writing and appeal. You can apply even if the bill already went to collections.
guide_apply.li1: Recent pay stubs (usually the last 1–3 months).
guide_apply.li2: Last year's tax return or W-2, if you have one.
guide_apply.li4: Proof of household size (for example, a tax return listing dependents).
guide_apply.li5: A short written statement if you have little or no income.
guide_apply.cta_p: Cobijo shows your hospital's exact income limits and writes your request letter — free, in your language.
guide_apply.nav_label: How to apply for charity care

guide_collections.title: Medical Bill in Collections in California? Know Your Rights | Cobijo Health
guide_collections.meta: A medical bill in collections in California doesn't mean you've lost your options. Know your rights, how medical debt affects your credit, and how to still get help.
guide_collections.h1: Medical bill in collections in California: your rights
guide_collections.lead: If a hospital bill has been sent to a collection agency, it can feel like it's too late to do anything. In California, it usually isn't. You still have strong rights — and you can still apply for financial assistance.
guide_collections.s1_h: You can still apply for financial assistance
guide_collections.s1_p: A bill going to collections does not cancel your right to charity care or a discount. Under California's Hospital Fair Pricing Act, you can apply for the hospital's financial-assistance program even after the account is in collections — and if you qualify, the hospital and its collection agency must adjust the balance. If you think you might qualify, apply before you pay or settle.
guide_collections.s2_h: Medical debt and your credit in California
guide_collections.s2_p: Under a 2024 California law (SB 1061, the Medical Debt Relief Act), medical debt generally cannot be reported to credit agencies or appear on your California credit report — and medical debt listed on a credit report is not enforceable against you. Separately, hospitals generally cannot report a bill to a credit agency or sell it until at least 180 days after the first bill (AB 1020).
guide_collections.s3_p: Debt collectors must follow the law. You can ask a collector, in writing, to verify that the debt is really yours and correct — and they must pause collection until they do. Collectors cannot harass, threaten, or lie to you. If a collector breaks these rules, you can complain to the California Attorney General or the federal Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. Keep records of every call and letter.
guide_collections.s4_p: Don't ignore the bill, but don't assume you have to pay the full amount either. First, check whether you qualify for the hospital's financial assistance — it may erase or shrink the balance. Ask for an itemized bill and check it for errors. If you do owe something, ask for an interest-free payment plan you can actually afford.
guide_collections.li1: You can apply for hospital charity care even after a bill is in collections.
guide_collections.li2: Medical debt can't be reported on your California credit report (SB 1061).
guide_collections.li3: Hospitals must wait at least 180 days before reporting or selling a bill (AB 1020).
guide_collections.li4: You can ask a collector, in writing, to verify the debt.
guide_collections.li5: Collectors cannot harass, threaten, or lie to you.
guide_collections.cta_h: See if you still qualify for help
guide_collections.cta_p: Even in collections, you may qualify for free or discounted care. Cobijo checks your hospital's rules and writes your letter — free.

guide_undoc.title: Hospital Bill Help for Undocumented Immigrants in California | Cobijo Health
guide_undoc.meta: Undocumented immigrants in California can get help with hospital bills: charity care doesn't require immigration status, Emergency Medi-Cal covers emergencies, and using it won't hurt your immigration case.
guide_undoc.h1: Hospital bill help if you're undocumented in California
guide_undoc.lead: If you're undocumented and worried about a hospital bill, you still have real options in California — and getting help usually will not affect your immigration case. Here's what you're entitled to and how to ask for it safely.
guide_undoc.s1_h: Charity care does not depend on your immigration status
guide_undoc.s1_p: Under California's Hospital Fair Pricing Act (Health & Safety Code §127400 and following), hospitals must offer free or discounted care based on your income and household size — not your immigration status. A hospital generally cannot require a Social Security number or proof of legal status to give you charity care, and if you're missing a document you can often provide a signed statement instead. Applying costs nothing.
guide_undoc.s2_h: Emergency and full-scope Medi-Cal, regardless of status
guide_undoc.s2_p: Emergency Medi-Cal (restricted-scope Medi-Cal) covers emergency and urgent care for income-eligible people regardless of immigration status, and it can be applied retroactively to a recent emergency. As of January 1, 2024, California also opened full-scope Medi-Cal to all income-eligible adults regardless of immigration status. If your income is low, it's worth checking whether Medi-Cal will cover the bill.
guide_undoc.s3_h: Getting help usually will not affect your immigration case
guide_undoc.s3_p: The federal “public charge” rule does not count most health coverage against you. Using Medi-Cal (except long-term care in an institution), emergency medical services, hospital charity care, or Covered California is generally not held against you in a green-card or visa decision. This is general information, not legal advice — if you have a specific case, a nonprofit immigration attorney can confirm what applies to you.
guide_undoc.s4_h: How Cobijo can help — free, private, in your language
guide_undoc.s4_p: Cobijo Health is a free, nonprofit tool. We don't ask your immigration status and we don't save anything you enter. Tell us your hospital, income, and household size, and we'll show whether you may qualify for free or discounted care there, screen you for Medi-Cal, and write you a request letter you can send — in your language.
guide_undoc.li1: Hospital charity care is based on income, not immigration status.
guide_undoc.li2: You generally don't need a Social Security number or proof of status to apply.
guide_undoc.li3: Emergency Medi-Cal covers emergencies regardless of status.
guide_undoc.li4: Using health care or Medi-Cal generally won't affect a public-charge decision.
guide_undoc.li5: Cobijo doesn't ask your status and saves nothing you enter.
guide_undoc.cta_h: See what help you may qualify for
guide_undoc.cta_p: Answer a few quick questions — no status asked, nothing saved — and get a personalized plan plus a ready-to-send letter, free and in your language.

guide_negotiate.title: How to Negotiate a Hospital Bill in California (Step by Step) | Cobijo Health
guide_negotiate.meta: You can negotiate a hospital bill in California. Ask for an itemized bill, check it for errors, apply for charity care first, ask for the uninsured discount, and set up an interest-free payment plan.
guide_negotiate.h1: How to negotiate a hospital bill in California
guide_negotiate.lead: A hospital bill is rarely the final word. In California you have the right to charity care, discounts, and a fair payment plan — and a few specific steps can lower what you actually owe. Here's how to negotiate, in order.
guide_negotiate.s1_h: Step 1: Apply for financial assistance first
guide_negotiate.s1_p: Before you negotiate a price, find out whether you owe anything at all. Under California's Hospital Fair Pricing Act, hospitals must give free or discounted care to eligible patients, and eligible patients cannot be charged more than the amount Medicare or Medi-Cal would pay. Charity care is a right, not a favor — so apply for it first. Cobijo shows your hospital's exact income limits.
guide_negotiate.s2_h: Step 2: Get an itemized bill and check it for errors
guide_negotiate.s2_p: Ask the billing office for a fully itemized bill that lists every charge. Billing errors are common — duplicate charges, services you never received, or the wrong billing code. Compare it to what you remember and question anything that looks wrong. Fixing an error can cut the bill before you negotiate anything else.
guide_negotiate.s3_h: Step 3: Ask for the uninsured or cash discount
guide_negotiate.s3_p: If you're uninsured or paying yourself, ask directly: “What is your discount for uninsured or self-pay patients?” Hospitals often have a standard uninsured discount, and the first price you're quoted is rarely the lowest. Be polite, be specific, and ask them to put any agreed discount in writing before you pay.
guide_negotiate.s4_h: Step 4: Set up a payment plan you can actually afford
guide_negotiate.s4_p: If you still owe a balance, ask for an interest-free monthly payment plan based on what you can realistically pay. Under California law, payment plans for eligible patients must be reasonable. Don't put the bill on a credit card or a medical credit card — that can turn protected medical debt into regular debt with interest. Get the plan in writing.
guide_negotiate.li1: Apply for charity care first — you may owe far less, or nothing.
guide_negotiate.li2: Get a fully itemized bill and dispute any errors.
guide_negotiate.li3: Ask for the uninsured or self-pay discount, in writing.
guide_negotiate.li4: Request an interest-free payment plan you can afford.
guide_negotiate.li5: Don't move the bill onto a credit card — keep it as medical debt.
guide_negotiate.cta_h: Start with what you may not owe
guide_negotiate.cta_p: Cobijo checks your hospital's charity-care rules and writes your request letter — free, in your language, nothing saved.
guide_negotiate.nav_label: How to negotiate a hospital bill

guide_er.title: Emergency Room Bill With No Insurance in California | Cobijo Health
guide_er.meta: Got an ER bill with no insurance in California? You can still apply for charity care, may qualify for retroactive Medi-Cal, and can't be charged more than Medicare rates if you're eligible. Here's what to do.
guide_er.h1: Emergency room bill with no insurance in California
guide_er.lead: An emergency room visit without insurance can lead to a frightening bill — but that bill is often reducible, and sometimes erased. Federal and California law give uninsured emergency patients real protections. Here's how to use them.
guide_er.s1_h: The ER had to treat you — and you can still get help paying
guide_er.s1_p: Under the federal EMTALA law, emergency rooms must screen and stabilize anyone with an emergency regardless of ability to pay or insurance status. Getting emergency care you couldn't pay for up front is normal and expected. Now that you have the bill, you can apply for the hospital's financial assistance — being uninsured is itself a common reason to qualify.
guide_er.s2_h: You may qualify for Medi-Cal — even retroactively
guide_er.s2_p: If your income is low, Medi-Cal may cover the visit. Medi-Cal can be applied retroactively to cover care in the three months before you applied, so an emergency you've already been billed for may still be covered. Many hospitals also offer Hospital Presumptive Eligibility, which can grant temporary Medi-Cal on the spot. Emergency Medi-Cal covers emergencies regardless of immigration status.
guide_er.s3_h: You can't be overcharged if you're eligible
guide_er.s3_p: Under California's Hospital Fair Pricing Act, eligible low-income and uninsured patients cannot be charged more than the amount Medicare or Medi-Cal would pay for the same care — often far less than the “sticker price” on the bill. Depending on the hospital, patients with household income up to 400% of the Federal Poverty Level (roughly $60,000 for one person or about $125,000 for a family of four) can qualify for a discount.
guide_er.s4_h: What to do with the bill now
guide_er.s4_p: Don't ignore it, and don't rush to pay the full amount. Apply for the hospital's financial assistance and check whether you qualify for Medi-Cal — either could erase or shrink the balance. Ask for an itemized bill and check it for errors. You can apply for help even if the bill is already old or in collections.
guide_er.li1: Apply for the hospital's financial assistance — uninsured patients often qualify.
guide_er.li2: Check Medi-Cal, including retroactive coverage for the past three months.
guide_er.li3: Ask whether the hospital offers Presumptive Eligibility for on-the-spot Medi-Cal.
guide_er.li4: If eligible, you can't be charged more than Medicare/Medi-Cal rates.
guide_er.li5: Get an itemized bill and dispute any errors.
guide_er.cta_h: See if your ER bill can be reduced
guide_er.cta_p: Answer a few quick questions and get a personalized plan plus a ready-to-send letter — free, in your language, nothing saved.

guide_surprise.title: Surprise Medical Bill in California? Your Protections | Cobijo Health
guide_surprise.meta: A surprise medical bill in California is often illegal. The federal No Surprises Act and California's AB 72 protect you from balance billing for out-of-network emergency care and providers. Here's how to dispute one.
guide_surprise.h1: Surprise medical bill in California: your protections
guide_surprise.lead: A “surprise” bill is one you get for out-of-network care you didn't choose — often after an emergency, or from a provider you didn't know was out-of-network. In California, many of these bills are limited or barred by law. Here's how to tell, and how to push back.
guide_surprise.s1_h: The No Surprises Act protects you in emergencies
guide_surprise.s1_p: Under the federal No Surprises Act (in effect since 2022), you generally cannot be balance-billed for out-of-network emergency care, or for out-of-network providers (like an anesthesiologist or radiologist) who treat you at an in-network hospital or facility. In those situations you should only owe your normal in-network cost-sharing — the same copay, coinsurance, or deductible you'd pay in-network.
guide_surprise.s2_p: California's AB 72 (in effect since 2017) protects patients in state-regulated health plans from surprise balance bills when they go to an in-network facility but are treated by an out-of-network provider. Between the federal and state laws, most Californians with insurance are protected from these surprise out-of-network charges — you owe the in-network amount, not the difference.
guide_surprise.s3_h: How to dispute a surprise bill
guide_surprise.s3_p: If you get a bill that looks like an illegal balance bill, don't pay the disputed amount right away. Call your health plan and the provider, say the bill appears to violate the No Surprises Act or AB 72, and ask them to reprocess it at the in-network rate. Keep notes of every call. You can also file a complaint with the No Surprises Help Desk or California's Department of Managed Health Care.
guide_surprise.s4_h: If you're uninsured or paying cash
guide_surprise.s4_p: If you don't use insurance, providers must give you a good-faith estimate of the cost in advance. If your final bill is at least $400 more than the estimate, you can use the federal patient-provider dispute resolution process to challenge it. And you can always apply for the hospital's charity care — being uninsured often qualifies you for free or discounted care.
guide_surprise.li1: You generally can't be balance-billed for out-of-network emergency care.
guide_surprise.li2: Out-of-network providers at an in-network facility can't surprise-bill you.
guide_surprise.li3: You should only owe your in-network cost-sharing amount.
guide_surprise.li4: Uninsured patients are owed a good-faith estimate in advance.
guide_surprise.li5: You can dispute a bill $400+ over the estimate, and apply for charity care.
guide_surprise.cta_h: Bill still too high? See if you qualify for help
guide_surprise.cta_p: Even after a surprise bill is corrected, Cobijo can check your hospital's charity-care rules and write your letter — free, in your language.
